coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode pH sensor with PMIA as substrate and CQDs as probe for high accuracy detection of full range pH
coredata.dc:creator: Huo, Kefeng
Zhang, Jifan
Lin, Tao
Zhang, Yiyun
Liu, Yi
Liu, Xunyong
coredata.dc:description: The pH test is particularly important in many fields, such as chemical production and medical diagnosis. However, the most commonly used pH test strips for pH detection have disadvantages such as low strength, poor stability, only qualitative measurement and only a single-color response. Therefore, a dual-mode pH sensor prepared by a phase conversion method with Poly(m-phenylene isophthalamide) (PMIA) as the substrate and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as probes was developed. This sensor is capable of producing dual changes in fluorescence color and fluorescence intensity over the full range of pH. The -COOH and -NH2 groups in CQDs can interact with the amide group in PMIA to make the sensor highly stable. Three correlations were established based on the fluorescence color and fluorescence intensity at different pH, which compensated the shortage of traditional pH test paper that could not be quantitatively detected. More importantly, the sensor can be used for pH detection in a wide range of water samples, and both colorimetric and fluorescence detection modes exhibit extremely high fit coefficients (R2 > 0.99) and short response times (∼5 s). Therefore, the dual-mode pH sensor provides an important solution for the development of pH detection devices with high stability and accuracy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Poly(m-phenylene isophthalamide)
Carbon quantum dots
pH
Dual-mode
Colorimetric
Fluorescent

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The optimization experiment of CQDs-PMIA sensor
The pH responsiveness testing experiment of the CQDs-PMIA sensor
Performance testing of CQDs-PMIA sensors and actual sample testing experiments
The optimization and anti-interference testing of CQDs-PMIA sensor
Fluorescence response of CQDs-PMIA sensors to pH
Colorimetric response of CQDs-PMIA sensors to pH
Cycle performance of sensors and actual sample testing
Comparison of the CQDs-PMIA sensor and traditional pH test paper

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS profiles of CQDs. (a) Total XPS profiles of CQDs. (b) High-resolution profiles of C1s. (c) High-resolution profiles of O1s; (d) High-resolution profiles of N1s.

(a) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CQDs. (b) High-resolution TEM images of CQDs (inset: fast Fourier transform mode). (c) Atomic force microscopy (AFM) photographs of CQDs. (d) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQDs. (e) Zeta potential of CQDs. (f) FTIR spectra of CQDs and sensor.

SEM images and AFM images of PMIA films and CQDs-PMIA sensors. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of (a) PMIA films and (d) CQDs-PMIA sensors. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of (b–c) PMIA films and (e–f) CQDs-PMIA sensors.
XPS profiles of CQDs-PMIA sensors. (a) Total XPS profiles of CQDs-PMIA sensors. (b) High-resolution profiles of C1s. (c) High-resolution profiles of O1s. (d) High-resolution profiles of N1s.

(a) Photographs of the CQDs-PMIA sensor under a UV lamp at a wavelength of 365 nm (left: pH = 5; right: pH = 9). (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CQDs-PMIA sensor with pH. (c) Fluorescence emission intensity of the CQDs-PMIA sensor with pH at a wavelength of 607 nm. (d) CIE coordinates of fluorescence emission spectra (calculated from fluorescence data).

(a) Fluorescence photos and split channels of the CQDs-PMIA sensor at different pH values (R: red channel; G: green channel; B: blue channel). (b) Fit of (R + G)/B versus pH. (c) CIE coordinates of color change.
(a) Segmental fit of R/B versus pH. (b) linear fit 1. (c) linear fit 2. (d) linear fit 3.
(a) Cycling performance of sensors: cycling performance of fluorescence detection mode of CQDs-PMIA sensor. (b) Cycling performance of colorimetric detection mode of CQDs-PMIA sensor.

Comparison of the CQDs-PMIA sensor and conventional pH paper. (a) The stability of the CQDs-PMIA sensor compared with conventional pH paper (left: conventional pH paper; mid: CQDs-PMIA sensor; right: CQDs-PMIA sensor under UV light). (b) The acid-base cycling performance of the CQDs-PMIA sensor compared with conventional pH paper (top: conventional pH paper; bottom: CQDs-PMIA sensor). (c) The cuttable performance of the CQDs-PMIA sensor is demonstrated. (d) The comparison of conventional pH test paper before and after stirring in liquid for 2 min. (e) The comparison of the CQDs-PMIA sensor before and after stirring in liquid for 2 min. (f) The tensile test graph of the CQDs-PMIA sensor and pH test paper.
Preparation process and photos of CQDs and CQDs-PMIA sensors.
Comparison of pH values of water samples detected by CQDs-PMIA sensors and commercial pH meters.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode pH sensor with PMIA as substrate and CQDs as probe for high accuracy detection of full range pH

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The pH test is particularly important in many fields, such as chemical production and medical diagnosis. However, the most commonly used pH test strips for pH detection have disadvantages such as low strength, poor stability, only qualitative measurement and only a single-color response. Therefore, a dual-mode pH sensor prepared by a phase conversion method with Poly(m-phenylene isophthalamide) (PMIA) as the substrate and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as probes was developed. This sensor is capable of producing dual changes in fluorescence color and fluorescence intensity over the full range of pH. The -COOH and -NH2 groups in CQDs can interact with the amide group in PMIA to make the sensor highly stable. Three correlations were established based on the fluorescence color and fluorescence intensity at different pH, which compensated the shortage of traditional pH test paper that could not be quantitatively detected. More importantly, the sensor can be used for pH detection in a wide range of water samples, and both colorimetric and fluorescence detection modes exhibit extremely high fit coefficients (R2 > 0.99) and short response times (∼5 s). Therefore, the dual-mode pH sensor provides an important solution for the development of pH detection devices with high stability and accuracy.

This dual mode sensor can detect the full range of pH.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the definition of pH was established at the beginning of the 19th century, there has been widespread research on pH detection [1]. The change in pH is a key indicator in chemical production, medical diagnostics, human metabolism and material corrosion [2–4]. Therefore, accurate pH detection is of great importance for many fields. In the past, pH testing has relied on pH meters and pH test strips. The advantage of a pH meter is its ability to accurately quantify pH. However, pH meters have long response times and have difficulty measuring small volume samples when measuring solutions with low buffering capacity [5,6], while the main structure of the glass electrode has the disadvantages of easy fouling, frequent calibration and fragility [7–9]. In view of the above deficiencies of pH meters, commercial pH test strips are the most widely used tools for testing pH and have the advantages of simple manufacturing, low cost and fast detection. However, current commercial pH test strips are limited in many applications due to their low strength, poor stability, qualitative measurement and single-color response [10–12]. Based on the above analysis, it is very important to build a test paper-type sensor that has the quantitative detection of a pH meter and can make up for many defects of traditional commercial pH test paper.

The majority of paper-based sensors consist of two main components: a matrix material that acts as a carrier and a detection probe that responds to pH. Traditional pH test paper uses ordinary paper as the substrate material, which directly leads to the low mechanical strength of pH test paper. Tomàs et al. developed a bandage-based wearable potential sensor for monitoring wound pH, which greatly enhanced the mechanical strength of the sensor [13]. However, it must be used with electrochemical analysis equipment, which makes it inconvenient to operate. Wang et al. developed a pH sensor with bromocresol green (BG)-doped silica film as a carrier, which has the disadvantage of a long preparation time and complicated preparation process [14]. Li et al. reported a proportional fluorescent pH sensor based on a sugar polymer, which has some mechanical strength but low stability due to the easy aging and denaturation of the sugar polymer. Among the many materials that can be used as a matrix, polymer matrix materials are the most widely used [15]. In particular, Poly(m-phenylene isophthalamide) (PMIA) is widely used in the manufacture of various film materials with excellent mechanical properties and hydrophilicity due to its special molecular and crystal structure and intermolecular hydrogen bonding [16]. However, the special molecular and crystalline structure of PMIA creates a smooth and inert surface, resulting in poor interfacial binding properties [17,18]. It is difficult to use PMIA with most probes to build a stable system, so it is less used in the field of sensing. According to the above analysis, PMIA would be very suitable as a substrate material for the sensor if a suitable detection probe can be designed and constructed.

In addition to the selection of suitable substrate materials, the selection of detection probes is also particularly important for building sensors with excellent performance. Most of the detection probes of conventional pH test strips use organic dyes. However, the most significant problem with the use of organic dyes as detection probes is the weak binding between the dye and the substrate, resulting in dye leaching [19,20], which makes the pH test strips less stable and reduces the cycling ability. To solve this issue, a paper-based sensor was prepared by Pourreza et al., using curcumin nanoparticles as detection probes, and treated with impregnated wax [21]. The effect of dye leaching was greatly improved with the sensor and the stability of the test paper was enhanced, but it could only detect in the pH = 7–13 range. Another problem with using organic dyes as detection probes is that there is only one colorimetric detection mode, resulting in a high probability of false-positives, which leads to less reliable test paper results [22]. To enhance the reliability of the assay results, Shuai et al. developed a surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS)-fluorescence dual-mode pH sensor based on Janus microgels for in vitro gastric fluid pH detection [23]. The dual-response property makes the detection results more reliable, but the use of precious metal ions to prepare the probes leads to higher costs. In recent years, many research teams have developed pH sensors based on carbon quantum dot (CQD) probes. CQDs have shown great potential as an emerging material in the field of pH sensing [24,25]. Compared with other detection probes, CQD probes have advantages such as low toxicity, easy synthesis, and low cost [26–28]. Researchers have developed a variety of CQD sensors based on fluorescence intensity, fluorescence intensity ratio and fluorescence lifetime [29–31]. The most widely developed sensors are based on fluorescence intensity. However, sensors based on fluorescence color change are less studied. Tao et al. developed a fluorescence intensity-based CQD sensor for the real-time sensing and detection of pH changes in human sweat during exercise processing [32]. Its shortcoming is that fluorescence intensity is the only signal response and is prone to false-positives. Wang et al. reported a dual-mode colorimetric/fluorescent sensor based on CQDs for the detection of pH and Cu2+ ions [33]. Although it has dual detection modes of colorimetric and fluorescence, the colorimetric mode can only measure qualitatively, and no quantitative detection system has been established. Li et al. reported a proportional fluorescent probe based on CQDs for intracellular pH monitoring and bioimaging with good biocompatibility and a quantitative detection system [34]. However, the detection range is only between pH = 3–8. Although a variety of sensors have been constructed around CQDs, there are few multi-response CQD probes capable of full-domain pH detection and establishing a quantitative detection system. Based on the above analysis, it is important to design CQDs with multiple responses for full-domain pH detection, quantitative detection and enhanced system stability through binding to a PMIA substrate.

To realize the above concept, a full-domain pH sensor with a dual response of fluorescence color and fluorescence intensity was constructed by stably combining CQDs and PMIA. The sensor has the advantage of quantitative pH meter detection but also makes up for the many shortcomings of traditional pH test paper. The formation of strong interactions between CQD probes and PMIA in the sensor increases the stability of CQD probes in the system, resulting in excellent cycling performance of the sensor. PMIA as a matrix not only provides excellent mechanical strength but also allows the sensor to have excellent permeability to protons, making the sensor response time extremely short (∼5 s). The excellent performance exhibited by the sensors makes them extremely promising for a variety of analytical applications. After testing, the sensor demonstrated extremely high accuracy (R2 > 0.99) and stability for pH detection. In summary, the colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode pH sensor with PMIA as the substrate and CQDs as the probe fills the gap in the current pH detection field and is expected to become a pH test strip that can be widely applied.

Terephthalic acid and o-phenylenediamine were purchased from Aladdin. Poly(m-phenylene isophthalamide) (PMIA) was purchased from Yantai Taihe New Material Co. Anhydrous lithium chloride was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent. Dimethyl sulfoxide was purchased from Tianjin Dingshengxin Chemical Co. The rest of the reagents were purchased from Aladdin.

CQDs were characterized using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Nicolet Nexus 470), X-ray diffractometry (XRD) (X′ Pert PRO MPD), Raman spectroscopy (Thermo Fischer DXR) and nanoparticle size and zeta potential analyzers (Zetasizer Nano ZS90). The morphology and dimensions of the CQDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL JEM 2100F,200 KV) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Bruker Dimension ICON). The elemental composition was characterized using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Fischer ESCALAB Xi+). The surface morphology of the CQDs-PMIA sensors was characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Hitachi Regulus8100) and atomic force microscopy (AFM).

The CQDs were synthesized by the one-pot method using terephthalic acid (PTA) and o-phenylenediamine (oPD) as carbon sources. In the experiment, 0.1 g PTA and 0.1 g oPD were added to 10 mL anhydrous ethanol and sonicated for 10 min. The mixture was transferred to an autoclave lined with 20 mL PTFE, held at 180 °C for 12 h and naturally cooled to room temperature. The product was filtered and then dialyzed in a dialysis bag (MW 100–500 Da) using ethanol for three days with a change of dialysate every 4 h. The final product is stored in the dark at 4 °C.

PMIA (10 g), 8 g LiCl and 74.5 mL DMSO were added to a three-neck flask and stirred continuously at 120 °C for 5 h until PMIA dissolved into a homogeneous yellow solution. The PMIA solution was composed of 10 wt% PMIA, 8 wt% LiCl and 82 wt% DMSO.

As shown in Scheme 1 , the above PMIA solution was weighed to 1.0 g and added to the beaker. Then, 0.25 mL CQDs was added and mixed well to obtain the CQDs-PMIA mixed solution. The mixed solution was applied to a clean glass plate using a film applicator and quickly immersed in a coagulation bath (100% water, room temperature). The film was removed when it precipitated and then rinsed with deionized water to remove the remaining solvent and LiCl. Finally, a CQDs-PMIA sensor was obtained by drying at 40 °C for 5 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimization experiment of CQDs-PMIA sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In the optimization experiments, we constructed six groups of sensors with concentrations of 0.1, 0.25, 0.40, 0.55, 0.70 and 0.85 ml/g, respectively. We analyzed and compared the changes of their fluorescence with pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The pH responsiveness testing experiment of the CQDs-PMIA sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, a buffer solution with pH = 1–14 was prepared (Table S1), and then the CQDs-PMIA sensors were placed into different pH buffer solutions and removed after 5 s.

Photographs of the fluorescence colors at different pH values were taken in a dark room equipped with a UV lamp of 365 nm wavelength using a smartphone, and then the R, G, B data were processed using Photoshop software to finally obtain the correlation between the fluorescence colors and pH.

The fluorescence emission intensity of a CQDs-PMIA sensor at different pH values with an excitation wavelength of 520 nm was measured using a fluorescence spectrometer. The correlation between the fluorescence intensity at the emission wavelength of 607 nm and pH was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance testing of CQDs-PMIA sensors and actual sample testing experiments
ce:section.ce:para: A CQDs-PMIA sensor was placed into a pH = 4 buffer solution, and the assay was recorded. Then, the sensor was rinsed with pure water, placed in pH = 10 buffer solution and rinsed with pure water after the assay. The test was repeated five times, and two groups were tested in parallel to obtain their test results in both modes and analyze their cycling performances.

The mechanical properties of the CQDs-PMIA sensors were analyzed by stretching, cutting and mechanical stirring experiments and compared with traditional pH test strips. The stability performance of the CQDs-PMIA sensors compared with traditional pH test strips was analyzed through experiments such as drip addition of buffer solution and cyclic detection of acid-base buffer solution.

A CQDs-PMIA sensor was used to detect four samples of rainwater, lake water, pure water and phosphate buffer solution. The obtained detection results were compared with those of commercial pH meters to test their accuracy in practical applications.

The elemental composition of CQDs was analyzed by XPS. The XPS pattern of the CQDs showed three peaks at 285, 532.5, and 399.6 eV, which corresponded to C, O, and N, respectively (Fig. 1 a). The elements were further analyzed by high-resolution XPS spectroscopy. The details of the high-resolution spectra of each element are shown in Fig. 1b–d. The high-resolution pattern of C1s can be fitted to three peaks at 284.8, 286.2, and 289.2 eV, which can be attributed to CC/C─C, C─N, and O─CO bonds. CC/C─C is derived from the graphene structure of CQDs [35,36]. The spectrum of O1s can be fitted to two peaks concentrated at 532.3 and 533.7 eV, which can be attributed to CO and C─O bonds [37]. The plot of N1s can be fitted as a peak at 399.6 eV, and this peak can be attributed to the C─N and N─H bonds [38]. The O─CO bond is derived from the carboxyl group in CQDs, and the C─N and N─H bonds are derived from the amino group in CQDs. Thus, the XPS mapping confirmed the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in CQDs, which laid the foundation for the detection of full domain pH.

The morphology of CQDs was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as shown in Fig. 2 a and b. The TEM images showed that the prepared CQDs were homogeneous and dispersed nanodots with a statistically derived average size of 3.07 nm. From the high-resolution images, the crystallinity structure of CQDs had a clear lattice stripe with a crystal plane spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (101) crystal plane of the graphite material [39]. This indicates that CQDs have a graphite-like structure. Its graphitic structure was reflected in the Raman spectra. The intensity ratio (IG/ID) of CQDs in the crystalline G-band and disordered D-band was 1.20 (Fig. S1a). The X-ray diffraction pattern was shown in Fig. S1b, which shows a peak around 49.2°, indicating a nearly identical lattice spacing for the CQDs. Fig. 2c shows the AFM image of CQDs with an average height of 1.3 nm, indicating that they consist of four to five layers of graphite [40].

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQDs are shown in Fig. 2d. The absorption peak at 297 nm corresponds to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains [41]. This is the unique structure of benzene and its derivatives. The absorption peak is red-shifted due to -NH2 and -COOH [42,43]. In addition, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum showed an absorption peak at 534 nm. The excitation spectrum showed two peaks at 520 nm and 554 nm. When λex = 520 nm, the maximum emission intensity is reached at λ = 607 nm. The zeta potential of CQDs were characterized using a nanoparticle size and zeta potential analyzer, as shown in Fig. 2e. The zeta potential was 9.27 mV. Although the zeta potential of the CQDs is not particularly high, but it can be kept relatively stable over time. As shown by the FTIR results of CQDs (Fig. 2f), CQDs have an absorption peak at 3200∼3400 cm−1, which is considered the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H bonds in the molecule [44]. The absorption peaks at 2973, 1645, and 1045 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–H, CO and C–O in CQDs [45–47]. The IR spectra also confirmed the presence of carboxyl and amino groups in CQDs, which is consistent with the XPS analysis. The CQDs-PMIA sensor shows a blueshift in the peak in the range of 850∼1100 cm−1, so it can be confirmed that hydrogen bonds are formed between CQDs and PMIA [48]. The hydrogen bonding contributes to the stability of the CQDs-PMIA sensor.

The surface structures of PMIA films and CQDs-PMIA sensors were characterized using SEM. The pure PMIA films had sparse surface pores (Fig. 3 a), while the surface of the CQDs-PMIA sensors exhibited more and larger pores (Fig. 3d). The reason for this change could be that the PMIA concentration was reduced by the dilution of the pure PMIA solution due to the addition of CQDs. In pure PMIA solutions, the higher concentration of PMIA long chain molecules and thus the tendency to entangle with each other leads to an increase in the viscosity of the solution, making it easier to form smaller pores during the phase transition. The viscosity of the system decreased after adding CQDs, the PMIA long-chain molecules had larger spaces between them, and the solvent diffused into the water during phase transformation, forming pores with larger diameters [49–51]. The capillary effect of these pores allows water molecules to be more readily absorbed by the sensor and greatly improves the sensor's water absorption. The surface morphologies of the PMIA films and CQDs-PMIA sensors were characterized using AFM, as shown in Fig. 3b–c and e-f. The surface Ra and Rq of the pure PMIA films were 5.05 and 6.23 nm, respectively, and the surface Ra and Rq of the CQDs-PMIA sensors were 4.76 and 3.64 nm, respectively. The CQDs-PMIA sensor shows a flatter surface with lower roughness compared to the PMIA film. Thus, the AFM results demonstrate the good compatibility of PMIA substrates with the designed CQDs.

The elemental composition of the CQDs-PMIA sensor was further analyzed using XPS. The figure shows three peaks (Fig. 4 a), located at 285, 400, and 532 eV, which are correspondingly attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s [52]. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s shows three peaks, of which the peak at 288.2 eV can be attributed to the O─CO bond (Fig. 4b). The O1s high-resolution spectrum can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.5 and 532.2 eV, corresponding to the CO and C─O bonds, respectively (Fig. 4c). The high-resolution spectrum of N1s fitted with a peak at 400.2 eV can be attributed to the C─N and N─H bonds, confirming the presence of -NH2 (Fig. 4d). The presence of -COO- and -NH2 confirmed the successful loading of CQDs onto PMIA substrates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimization and anti-interference testing of CQDs-PMIA sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence performance and color change performance of the sensor are mainly influenced by the internal CQD probes. Therefore, the amount of CQDs added during the preparation of the sensor will affect the sensor performance. To screen the best concentration, we tried various concentration ratios to make the sensor and test its performance. We fixed the amount of PMIA substrate and varied the volume of CQDs added to explore the optimal concentration of CQDs in the sensor. As shown in Fig. S2, when the concentration is 0.1 ml/g, the low concentration causes the sensor to have a color change only in the pH = 1–4 range. When pH > 4, the color change of the sensor is similar and unsuitable for colorimetric analysis. The sensors of the other four concentrations all have a clear color change in full pH range: from purple gradually to pale yellow. Therefore, we excluded the sensor with a concentration of 0.10 ml/g. We fitted the RGB data of sensors with concentrations of 0.25, 0.40, 0.55, 0.70 and 0.85 ml/g (Fig. S3∼S4). The results show that a concentration of 0.25 ml/g gives the best fit (Table S2). In addition, the 0.25 ml/g is also the best choice based on the aspect of saving raw materials. Therefore, we chose 0.25 ml/g as the best concentration.

we explored the effects of various ions on the CQDs-PMIA sensor. In the anti-interference experiments, we investigated the effects of various common cations (Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Li+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Ag+, K+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Mn2+) and anions (CH3COO−, S2O4 2−, CO3 2−, HPO4 2−, NO3 −, Cl−) on the sensor. As shown in Fig. S5, the CQDs-PMIA sensor exhibited stable fluorescence intensity under different ionic environments. This indicates that other substances in the water do not affect the CQDs-PMIA sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CQDs-PMIA sensors to pH
ce:section.ce:para:
We investigated the pH response performance of the prepared CQDs-PMIA sensors. Fig. 5 a shows a photograph of the sensor under a UV lamp (365 nm), which can be seen to exhibit distinctly different colors in acidic and alkaline environments. We specifically examined the variation of the CQDs-PMIA sensor with pH in the pH range between 1 and 14 and obtained the fluorescence emission spectrum of the sensor at an excitation wavelength of 520 nm in the full range of pH (Fig. 5b). The graph shows that the fluorescence emission spectrum of the CQDs-PMIA sensor shows an emission peak at 607 nm, and the intensity of the spectrum at 607 nm gradually increases with increasing pH and reaches a maximum at pH = 13. However, the fluorescence emission intensity showed a decrease at pH = 14. The extremely high pH may have a Quenching effect on the fluorescence [53,54]. The variation in the fluorescence emission spectrum at 607 nm with pH is shown in Fig. 5c, where it can be observed that the fluorescence emission intensity of the sensor increases from 1000 to approximately 9000 as the pH increases from 1 to 13, which shows a drop of approximately 8000 between the two platforms. A nonlinear fit using the Boltzmann equation yielded an "S" shaped fit curve between pH and fluorescence emission intensity (R2 = 0.9924). The specific formula is
y = 8.72 ×
10 3
−
7.67 ×
10 3
/
( 1 + exp ⁡
(
(
x − 7.49
)
/ 0.71
)
)
. This means that the fluorescence emission intensity of the sensor can respond to pH changes according to the correlation of the specific equation, and the pH of the solution can be accurately read by reading the fluorescence emission intensity at 607 nm. The CIE coordinates corresponding to the fluorescence intensity of the sensor show a shift from the green region to the red region (Fig. 5d). This indicates that the fluorescence emission wavelength shifts from short to long wavelengths, and this shift lays the foundation for the colorimetric change of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric response of CQDs-PMIA sensors to pH
ce:section.ce:para:
As a device that can be applied in the field for rapid inspection, its color change performance and response speed are extremely important. Therefore, the fluorescence color change of the sensor under different pH stimuli was tested. The sensor exhibited different fluorescent colors in buffer solutions with pH = 1–14, and the color change time was approximately 5 s. The photograph in Fig. 6 a was taken using a smartphone in a dark room equipped with a violet light at a wavelength of 365 nm. The photograph shows that the color of the sensor gradually changed as the pH increased. The qualitative judgment of pH by observing the color change was realized. In addition, the fluorescent photos of the sensor can be analyzed using Photoshop software to convert the sensor color at different pH values to R, G, and B data, and the data can be used to quantitatively represent the color change of the sensor [55]. The correlation between (R + G)/B and pH was found by splitting the color channels of the photomicrographs to obtain the intensity variation of each channel of R, G and B. An "S"-shaped correlation curve was then fitted using the Boltzmann equation. The correlation between (R + G)/B and pH showed a strong correlation (R2 = 0.9991) (Fig. 6b), and the pH could be quantified based on this fitted equation. Fig. 6c shows the position of the fluorescence color of the sensor in the CIE coordinate system and the trend of the change. The chromaticity coordinates (x, y) of the fluorescence color variation are calculated from Eqs. (1)–(3) [56]

0.01000
0.99000
]
[
R
G
B
]
(2)
x =
X /
(
X + Y + Z
)
(3)
y =
Y /
(
X + Y + Z
)

First, the obtained R, G and B are transformed by the normalized matrix, from which the XYZ tristimulus values are calculated. Then, the two chromaticity coordinates (x, y) are calculated from the XYZ tristimulus values by Equations (2) and (3).

In the above analysis, both the curve fitted to the fluorescence emission intensity versus pH and the curve fitted to (R + G)/B versus pH showed an "S" shape. However, since the rate of change at the two plateaus of the "S" curve is low, there is a tendency for large measurement errors to occur when measuring too high an acid/base. To improve the measurement accuracy of the sensor, a second correlation between R, G and B data and pH was designed (Fig. 7 a), and the fitted curve in the figure shows the form of three segments. The first segment ranged from pH = 1–5 and R1 2 = 0.9955 (Fig. 7b). The second segment ranged from pH = 5–9 and R2 2 = 0.9967 (Fig. 7c). The third segment ranged from pH = 9–14 and R3 2 = 0.9939 (Fig. 7d). The equation is shown in Table S3. Because Line 1 and Line 2 have partially identical R/B values, the segmentation model cannot be used directly to derive the solution pH without knowing the overall situation of the solution. The analysis was then combined with the "S" model, whereby the "S" model was first used to qualitatively test the sample, and then the segmentation model was used to precisely determine the pH value. The segmented fitting model is a complementary analysis method to compensate for the shortcomings of the "S" model and further improve the detection accuracy of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cycle performance of sensors and actual sample testing
Comparison of the CQDs-PMIA sensor and traditional pH test paper
ce:section.ce:para: As a pH sensor with great application potential, in addition to accurate pH detection accuracy, its mechanical performance and stability performance are also particularly important. To fully investigate the performance of CQDs-PMIA sensors, a series of evaluation experiments were conducted by selecting conventional pH test strips as a control. The leaching of the probe was first examined by adding drops of the same buffer solution to the pH paper and the sensor, then observing the leaching of the probe after a few seconds and comparing them. The results are shown in Fig. 9 a. It was observed that the probe leaching of the conventional pH test paper was extremely severe, while no probe leaching was observed for the CQDs-PMIA sensor. It was confirmed that the sensor was more stable than conventional pH test paper. The cycling performance of the two was also compared, and five acid-base cycles were performed for both the conventional pH test paper and the sensor. The test results are shown in Fig. 9b. The sensor maintained stable performance after five acid-base cycles, while the conventional pH paper lost its ability to detect pH after one test. This confirms the superior cycling performance of the sensor compared to conventional pH paper. In addition, as shown in Fig. 9c, the CQDs-PMIA sensors had excellent cuttable properties and could be made into various shapes according to practical applications. Then, the mechanical strength of the pH paper and CQDs-PMIA sensors were compared. The pH paper and sensors were placed in beakers with pure water, and magnets were added and stirred at the same speed for 5 min. The results are shown in Fig. 9d and e. The conventional pH test paper broke into a flocculent form after stirring, and the dye dissolved in water, while the sensor was very stable without any breakage after stirring in the beaker. Meanwhile, we tested the tensile performance of the pH test paper and sensor, and the results are shown in Fig. 9f. Both the strength and toughness of CQDs-PMIA sensors were stronger than those of traditional pH test strips. The evaluation of the CQDs-PMIA sensor and pH test paper shows that the performance of the CQDs-PMIA sensor is far better than that of traditional pH test paper, which can compensate for the shortcomings of traditional pH test paper, such as poor stability and low mechanical strength.

In this study, a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode pH sensor was prepared by adding CQDs to a PMIA solution using a phase conversion method to successfully load CQDs onto PMIA. As the pH changes, the sensor exhibits a series of changes in fluorescence color and fluorescence intensity, developing a visual detection method based on a dual mode of colorimetry and fluorescence. The sensor demonstrated extremely high resolution (R2 > 0.99) in both modes (colorimetric and fluorescent). In tests on actual water samples, the sensor showed an accuracy comparable to that of commercial pH meters (≤0.2 pH). Compared with pH test strips, the sensors we constructed are extremely stable and can be used for continuous monitoring in water with excellent cycling performance. In addition, PMIA-based sensors have higher mechanical strength and toughness than paper-based sensors. The CQDs-PMIA sensor reported in this paper provides a new fast, convenient, low-cost and high-precision method and material for pH detection in common water samples. Meanwhile, due to its excellent mechanical properties, stability and plasticity, it can be used for system-pH detection alone and is also expected to be applied to the development of intelligent detection equipment by combining with intelligent instruments.

Kefeng Huo: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Validation. Jifan Zhang: Data curation, Resources, Validation. Tao Lin: Visualization, Investigation. Yiyun Zhang: Software, Validation. Yi Liu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Xunyong Liu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52073134), Shandong Province Natural Science Foundation (ZR2023MB024, ZR2019MEM026), Yantai Key R&D Program (2020XDRH098), the Science Fund of Shandong Laboratory of Advanced Materials and Green Manufacturing (Yantai) (AMGM2023A02) and the Science Fund of Yantai Growth Drivers Conversion Research Institute and Yantai Science and Technology Achievement Transfer and Transformation Demonstration Base (KXDNY2023-1).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2023.111574.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent paper-based analytical devices for ultra-sensitive dual-type RNA detections and accurate gastric cancer screening

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.Q. Liu, R.Y. Li, F.Y. Liang, C. Deng, F. Seidi, H.N. Xiao, Fluorescent paper-based analytical devices for ultra-sensitive dual-type RNA detections and accurate gastric cancer screening, Biosens Bioelectron, 197(2022) 113781.
